title: Persistent currents in signed directed networks
abstract: Network theory can be fruitfully used to describe quantum coherence in physical systems. To that purpose we introduce persistent currents in signed directed networks by interpreting the signed magnetic Laplacian as an effective Hamiltonian and the associated edge phases as a discrete gauge field. In a canonical ensemble, persistent currents arise as thermodynamic responses to variations of gauge-invariant fluxes. We show that these fluxes are naturally defined on the cycle space of the network, and that the resulting currents are constrained to the divergence-free subspace and decompose onto independent cycles. This formulation provides a direct generalization of persistent currents from rings and lattices to arbitrary topologies. Detection of persistent currents provides a signature of the quantum phase coherence supported by the network, and a direct signature of the geometry of its cycle space. Such a mapping, not only allows a practical way to deal with quantum coherence for a variety of situations in the field of quantum technologies, but it also allows a physical interpretation of the importance of the Laplacian operator in graph theory, linking its role to the one of Hamiltonian (i.e. a tight-binding one) in physical systems. To test the power of the method, we construct a signed directed network that reproduces the Hofstadter butterfly spectrum.
summary: This work develops a generalized theory for persistent currents on arbitrary signed directed networks by treating the system thermodynamically. It maps quantum coherence onto fluxes defined over the network's cycle space, providing a topological signature of phase coherence. The framework is powerful enough to reproduce complex spectral features like the Hofstadter butterfly.
main_problem: To generalize the concept of persistent currents from simple rings/lattices to arbitrary signed directed networks using a thermodynamic framework.
main_result: Persistent currents are shown to be divergence-free equilibrium currents that decompose onto the cycle basis, providing a topological signature of quantum phase coherence in complex networks.
method: The analysis uses a Gibbs ensemble approach, defining persistent currents as thermodynamic responses (derivatives of the free energy) to gauge-invariant fluxes defined on the network's cycle space.
model_or_system: The system is modeled by signed directed networks, where edge phases act as a discrete gauge field. The dynamics are governed by an effective Hamiltonian derived from the signed magnetic Laplacian, which is treated in a canonical ensemble.
key_observables: Global persistent current $J(q)$, cycle fluxes $\Psi$, and the response kernel $K(	au)$ relating currents to fluxes.
important_parameters: Inverse temperature/diffusion time ($	au$), flux parameters ($q$), and the network topology (cycle space geometry).
main_assumptions: The analysis is performed in an equilibrium setting, using a free energy expansion around zero flux. The method successfully reproduces known results for rings and lattices.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate persistent currents in looped networks across different cycle numbers, showing the corresponding spectra of the signed magnetic Laplacian and the decomposition onto independent cycle modes.
paper_structure: The paper develops a thermodynamic framework by introducing gauge-invariant fluxes on the cycle space. It derives linear relations between current responses and these fluxes using the free energy expansion, culminating in an application to reproduce the Hofstadter butterfly spectrum.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful topological tool (cycle space analysis) to characterize quantum coherence signatures in complex graph structures, linking spectral properties (Laplacian eigenvalues) directly to transport phenomena.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Persistent currents in looped networks. (a) Current as a function of q for a single-cycle network (inset), showing periodic behavior. (b) Total current for a network with two independent cycles. (c,d) Corresponding spectra of the signed magnetic Laplacian. (e,f) Decomposition of the current onto the two cycle modes at different τ, highlighting interference and multiscale contributions. Weights are color coded in blue (negative) and red (positive). Transparency codifies for am- plitude.

Figure 2. Hofstadter network. (a) Energy spectrum computed on a network with N = 400 nodes as a function of q, show- ing the butterfly-like fractal structure. (b) Representative signed directed network with spatially structured asymmetric weights, shown for a smaller system with N = 25 nodes for vi- sualization purposes. (c) Global persistent current computed on the full N = 400 network. (d) Cycle-resolved currents computed on the same N = 400 network.
